danger_signals.visual_form: Ancient mechanical entity with immense power
danger_signals.meaning: World-threatening antagonist force; source of magical corruption and danger
Consequence of releasing Dal Gren; signals magnitude of threat and loss of safe haven

danger_signals.visual_form: Political/military authority attempting to weaponize Dal Gren
danger_signals.meaning: Human antagonist faction posing secondary threat through authoritarian power
Environmental hostility; unpredictable danger in exploration spaces

safety_signals.visual_form: Settled community spaces on overworld map
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zones for healing, mercenary recruitment, and inn rest
Mechanical and narrative safe point for resource recovery

safety_signals.visual_form: NPC characters available for hire in towns
safety_signals.meaning: Progression safety through party expansion and combat support

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Military/authoritarian enemy types aligned with Zaygos
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Areas under dictatorial control; locations where Zaygos exerts political power
Magic school (destroyed); represents legitimate magical authority and knowledge source
Towns and recruitment hubs; mercenaries have individual spirit loadouts distinguishing their capabilities

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Magic school intact; world order stable
Magic school destroyed; world under threat
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Urgency in NPC dialogue; recruitment of mercenaries; focus on Dal Gren destruction quest

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dictatorial authority expanding control; locations contested between regime and player allies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Zaygos attempting to weaponize Dal Gren; military encounters; political intrigue through mercenary and NPC interactions

environmental_storytelling.example: Magic school's ruined state communicates the catastrophic impact of Dal Gren's awakening without exposition
Headmaster of magic school orders Chezni to destroy Dal Gren, establishing world stakes through character authority
Stronger mercenaries become available as player progresses, signaling world danger escalation and alliance-building opportunities
Towns, dungeons, and contested spaces visually represent zones of safety, danger, and factional influence
Player's choice to hire specific mercenaries signals alliance with different power centers and skill traditions

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Travels through world seeking Dal Gren destruction; accepts allies and mercenaries
Joins party voluntarily to aid Chezni on his quest
Join party for payment; retain independent equipment and spirit loadouts; some possess rare spells unavailable to main characters
Attempts to weaponize ancient machine for personal authoritarian goals
Poses existential danger; source of magic system conflict (spells cost HP); motivates entire quest
Trigger unpredictable encounters in dungeons and overworld; scale in difficulty as player progresses

notes: Paladin's Quest uses science-fantasy setting (Lennus: Memories of an Ancient Machine) where political threat (Zaygos's dictatorship) intersects with existential threat (Dal Gren). Visual communication relies heavily on architectural markers (destroyed magic school), NPC dialog authority (headmaster's command), and overworld geography (towns as safe nodes vs. dungeons as danger). The magic system itself (spells cost HP) communicates world danger through mechanical resource scarcity. Faction identity is weak in visual differentiation; factions appear primarily through narrative authority and NPC allegiance rather than sigils or color schemes. World-state progression is communicated through mercenary availability escalation and NPC dialogue urgency rather than dramatic visual environmental changes.
